fixtures.scenarioDescription: please review codex/timeline-bundle-unification using @docs/CODE_REVIEW.md use subagents and then verify their comments…

timelineRows.text:
please review codex/timeline-bundle-unification using @docs/CODE_REVIEW.md

use subagents and then verify their comments before you synthesize the final report

All major claims verified. Synthesizing the final report now.

---

# Code Review: `codex/timeline-bundle-unification`

73 commits, ~17k lines net change. Major rewrite of the timeline display model: single semantic projection feeding React, CLI, and audit serializers; removal of `thread-detail-rows.ts`, `timeline-assistant-step-summary.ts`, `OperationRow.tsx`, `ConversationEntry.tsx`, etc. Reviewed independently by four agents against the merge base `cdc9566e86`.

## Top-priority issues

### 1. Approval state silently dropped on exploring-classified commands (BUG)
`packages/core-ui/src/timeline-source-projection.ts:459-499`. `convertCommandExecutionMessage` and `convertToolCallMessage` both call `convertExploringActivity` first; if the message classifies as exploration (read/search/list intents), the resulting work rows carry `status` only — `approvalStatus` is dropped on the floor. A pending-approval `cat secrets.txt` becomes an innocuous "Read secrets.txt" with no waiting indicator. The non-exploring branches preserve `approvalStatus` correctly. **No regression test** for this path in `timeline-display-model.test.ts`. Fix by threading `approvalStatus` into the exploring intent rows or by gating the exploring path on `approvalStatus === null`.

### 2. Error `code` no longer surfaces when not in `KNOWN_ERROR_COPY` (regression)
`packages/core-ui/src/timeline-source-projection.ts:355-367` + `packages/core-ui/src/error-display.ts:22-34, 89-100`. Previously `detail = message.detail ?? message.code`. Now `buildErrorDisplay` uses `code` only as a key into a hardcoded copy table; if there's no detail and the code isn't in the table, `code` is invisible to the user. Debuggability regression for any provider error code not enumerated.

### 3. `includeNestedRows: false` only strips top-level turn children (BUG)
`apps/server/src/services/threads/timeline.ts:211-227`. The map only blanks `children` on top-level `turn` rows. Delegations recursively call `buildTimelineRowsFromProjection`, so `TimelineDelegationWorkRow.childRows` still ships the full subagent tree. The flag's name implies it strips nesting; in practice it strips only the outer layer. Either rename the flag to `includeTurnChildren` or recursively apply the strip. No test exercises this for delegation-bearing threads.

### 4. `MemoizedTimelineRowEntry` memo is broken (PERF-RENDER)
`packages/ui-core/src/thread-timeline/NestedTimelineRows.tsx:761-780`. Every entry receives `rows={rows}` — the full sibling array. The prop is unused inside `TimelineRowEntry` but the new array reference on every parent render busts `React.memo`'s shallow equality, so every sibling re-renders on every WebSocket tick. Combined with `useMemo(() => getTimelinePresentationRow(row), [row])` at line 685 — `row` is rebuilt fresh by the projection on each tick — every row's presentation tree is rebuilt every tick. On streaming, long threads this is expensive. Drop the `rows` prop; consider hoisting presentation building to a single pass keyed by row id.

### 5. `?? 0` fallbacks contradict required schema (AGENTS-VIOLATION)
`apps/server/src/routes/threads/data.ts:123-126` vs `packages/server-contract/src/api-types.ts:785-789`. Schema declares `sourceSeqStart`/`sourceSeqEnd` as required `z.string().regex(/^\d+$/)`. Route then does `parseOptionalInteger(query.sourceSeqStart) ?? 0`. Dead fallback at the boundary, exactly the "optional as default-hider" pattern AGENTS.md forbids. Remove the `?? 0`s.

## Maintainability / shortcuts

### 6. `getWorkSemanticKind` is duplicated identically in two files
`packages/core-ui/src/timeline-presentation.ts:184-204` and `packages/core-ui/src/timeline-row-groups.ts:210-230`. Same package, same body. Extract to a shared helper — both must update together when a new `workKind` is added.

### 7. Format-then-strip rendered text
`packages/core-ui/src/timeline-presentation.ts:367-396`. `buildExplorationBundleLabel` calls `formatExplorationSummary(...)` then `summary.slice("explored ".length)` to recover the body; same for `buildWebResearchBundleLabel` stripping `"researched "`. Classic AGENTS.md anti-pattern: string manipulation of structured data. The summary builder should return verb and body separately.

### 8. ~200 lines of dead code in `rows/shared.tsx`
`packages/ui-core/src/thread-timeline/rows/shared.tsx`. Verified by grep — `SlowToolHeader`, `TimelineStaticRow`, `formatSummaryDuration`, `getSummaryDurationMs`, `useLiveNow`, `getElapsedDurationMs`, `getStaticEventToneClass`, `OngoingEventLabel`, `EventTitle` and their associated types have no callers anywhere in the repo. Delete them.

### 9. Synthetic file-change row uses `callId` as `path`
`packages/core-ui/src/timeline-source-projection.ts:505-528`. When `changes.length === 0` and an `approvalStatus` is set, the converter manufactures a row with `path: message.callId`, `displayName: "file changes"`. `countUniqueFileChangePaths` (`timeline-presentation.ts:273-277`) treats the path as identity — a callId leaks into a deduplication set. Fix the upstream `ViewFileEditMessage` to carry path metadata when it carries approval state, instead of synthesizing a fake row downstream.

### 10. ID namespacing is a structural workaround
`packages/core-ui/src/timeline-source-projection.ts:162-255`. Six functions exist purely to rewrite delegation child IDs so they don't collide with parent rows. Cleaner: derive timeline row IDs as `${parentChain}:${messageId}` from the start. Currently every new TimelineRow variant must be added in three places (type union, `buildPresentationRow`, `namespaceTimelineRow`) with no compiler help.

### 11. `Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER` sentinel for ordering
`packages/core-ui/src/timeline-row-groups.ts:443-448`. Pending steers are appended with `sourceSeqStart: Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER` to force them to sort last. Sentinel hiding missing data — give pending steers a real bucket or make the entry list two-phase.

### 12. `tasks` projection state half-removed
`packages/core-ui/src/timeline-source-projection.ts:72-75` (commit 99c9fd6ea) dropped `tasks` from `TimelineProjectionState`, but `ViewProjectionState.tasks` still exists in `packages/domain/src/timeline-projection.ts`. Either consume it or remove from upstream.

## Test coverage

### 13. ~50 unit tests deleted in favor of ~16 integration-style tests
Deleted: `format-timeline-text.test.ts` (16), `thread-detail-rows.test.ts` (25), `timeline-cli-rendering.snapshots.test.ts` (10), `thread-detail-activity.test.ts` (7), `timeline-assistant-step-summary.test.ts` (3). New: `timeline-display-model.test.ts` (9), `timeline-source-projection.test.ts` (3). The new suite exercises the full pipeline well, but two specific behaviors lack obvious replacements: (a) consecutive reconnect-error collapsing, (b) single-tool turn expansion. Worth confirming the negative checks in `timeline-display-model.test.ts` cover these.

### 14. No regression test for issues 1, 2, 3
Bug fixes from sections 16f85150c ("Keep file-change approvals visible") and fbfc61269 ("Keep ordinary file-change labels stable") landed without targeted tests for the exploring-approval path.

### 15. Snapshot churn (~14k lines) is justified
`packages/agent-provider-audit/test/__snapshots__/replay-fixtures.test.ts.snap`. The accompanying `replay-fixtures.test.ts:62-75` adds explicit invariants ("no `tool-exploring` view-message kinds remain"), demonstrating the regen is intentional rather than rubber-stamped.

## Plan adherence

Plan: `plans/timeline-display-model-rewrite.md`. Implementation matches scope. Two deviations:
- Plan's `role` vocabulary (`mono`/`danger`/`diff-add`/`diff-remove`) was replaced by `tone` (`default-muted`/`diff-added`/`diff-removed`/`emphasis`/`most-muted`). No `mono`/`danger`. Markup tag names diverged from the plan's documented set. Functional but undocumented.
- Plan §"Auditability Gates" requires that `apps/server/src/scripts/audit-timeline-dev-db.ts` "has been run and compared." No fixture artifact is committed; reviewers cannot machine-verify the exit criterion.

## Security & performance (lower-impact)

- **Security: clean.** No new authorization gaps, no new injection vectors. `previousParentName`/`nextParentName` (`base.ts:175-188`) reads thread titles via `parentThreadId` from the patch payload — fine under the project's single-tenant trust boundary.
- **Perf-scale: pre-existing.** `buildThreadTimeline` (`timeline.ts:237-242`) loads every event for the thread. Not introduced here, not addressed here. Worth follow-up now that turns are first-class.
- **Perf-N+1: minor.** `getThreadOwnershipDisplayName` does two separate `getThread` lookups in `base.ts:179-187`. Trivial to batch.
- **Index added:** `events_thread_turn_sequence_idx (thread_id, turn_id, sequence)` directly supports `listStoredTurnScopedEventRowsByTurnIds`. Reasonable.

## Recommendations, ordered

1. Fix issue 1 (approval state lost on exploring) before merge — silent security-adjacent regression.
2. Fix issues 2, 3, 5 — small, high-value.
3. Fix issue 4 — material rendering perf regression on streaming threads.
4. Address issues 6–9 in same change (deduplication, dead code, format-then-strip, synthetic path).
5. Address issues 10–12 as follow-ups (refactor ID namespacing, sentinel ordering, dead `tasks` state).
6. Add regression tests covering #1, #2, #3, the deleted-coverage gaps in #13, and the manager-thread `includeNestedRows` carve-out.

fixtures.scenarioDescription: Provider-targeted eyedropper browser compatibility task against the real Excalidraw repo

timelineRows.text: I want to make Excalidraw's eyedropper experience friendlier on browsers with uneven platform support. Please inspect the current repo behavior and use the built-in WebSearch and WebFetch tools directly to verify the current browser/platform docs around the EyeDropper API before you recommend a change. Keep a todo list as you go and avoid delegating this research to a subagent.

---

## Analysis & Recommendation

### Current state of the repo

Excalidraw implements a **fully custom, canvas-based eyedropper** in `EyeDropper.tsx`. It does **not** use the browser-native `window.EyeDropper` API at all. Instead it:

1. Overlays a transparent backdrop (`excalidraw-eye-dropper-backdrop`) over the canvas.
2. On `pointermove`, reads the pixel under the cursor via `ctx.getImageData(…)` from the Excalidraw canvas.
3. Shows a small color preview `div` that follows the cursor.
4. On `pointerup`, commits the picked color.

This means Excalidraw's eyedropper **can only sample colors from within the Excalidraw canvas itself** — it cannot sample from the OS desktop, other browser tabs, or even from the Excalidraw UI chrome (toolbars, panels, etc.).

The eyedropper button is rendered in `ColorInput.tsx` (line 99–131) and is already hidden on phone form factors, but there is **zero** feature-detection or user-facing messaging about the browser-native `EyeDropper` API.

### Browser-native `EyeDropper` API support (verified today)

| Browser | Supported? | Since |
|---------|-----------|-------|
| Chrome (desktop) | ✅ | v95 |
| Edge (desktop) | ✅ | v95 |
| Opera (desktop) | ✅ | v81 |
| Firefox | ❌ | Bug [#1728527](https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=1728527) — UNCONFIRMED, P3, unassigned for 4+ years |
| Safari | ❌ | Bug [#229755](https://bugs.webkit.org/show_bug.cgi?id=229755) — WebKit standards position still "Needs position" |
| All mobile browsers | ❌ | No support anywhere |

**Bottom line:** ~33% global coverage (Chromium-desktop only). Firefox has no implementation timeline. WebKit has not committed to it and has flagged design concerns.

### Recommended change: Progressive enhancement with native API + canvas fallback

The approach: **use the native `EyeDropper` API on supported browsers** (which lets users pick colors from *anywhere* on screen), and **keep the existing canvas-based implementation as a fallback** for Firefox, Safari, and mobile.

Here's the concrete plan:

#### 1. Add a capability-detection helper

```ts
// packages/excalidraw/components/EyeDropper.tsx (or a new util)
export const supportsNativeEyeDropper = (): boolean =>
  typeof window !== "undefined" && "EyeDropper" in window;
```

#### 2. Add a native-API path in `EyeDropper.tsx`

When `supportsNativeEyeDropper()` is `true`, short-circuit the existing canvas-pixel-reading logic and instead call the browser API:

```ts
// Inside the EyeDropper component's useEffect
if (supportsNativeEyeDropper()) {
  const nativeDropper = new (window as any).EyeDropper();
  const abortController = new AbortController();

  nativeDropper
    .open({ signal: abortController.signal })
    .then((result: { sRGBHex: string }) => {
      stableProps.onSelect(result.sRGBHex, /* synthetic event */);
    })
    .catch((err: Error) => {
      // User pressed Escape or aborted
      if (err.name !== "AbortError") {
        console.warn("EyeDropper failed, falling back to canvas picker", err);
      }
      stableProps.onCancel();
    });

  return () => abortController.abort();
}
// … existing canvas-based logic as fallback …
```

#### 3. Update the tooltip / label to reflect the limitation

In `ColorInput.tsx`, differentiate the title text:

```ts
title={supportsNativeEyeDropper()
  ? `${t("labels.eyeDropper")} — ${KEYS.I.toUpperCase()} …`
  : `${t("labels.eyeDropperCanvasOnly")} — ${KEYS.I.toUpperCase()} …`}
```

And add a new locale key in `en.json`:

```json
"eyeDropperCanvasOnly": "Pick color from canvas (browser-wide picking not supported)"
```

#### 4. (Optional) Show a one-time educational toast on unsupported browsers

When a Firefox/Safari user first activates the eyedropper, show a brief toast:
> "Color picking is limited to the canvas. For screen-wide picking, use Chrome or Edge."

This avoids confusion when users expect the picker to work outside the canvas (a common expectation set by design tools like Figma).

### Why this approach?

| Concern | How it's addressed |
|---------|--------------------|
| Chrome/Edge users get a better UX | Native API picks from *anywhere* on screen |
| Firefox/Safari still work | The existing canvas-pixel reader remains as the fallback |
| No broken buttons | The eyedropper icon always appears; only the *scope* of picking differs |
| Future-proof | When Firefox/Safari ship the API, users automatically get the upgrade |
| Mobile | Already gated by `formFactor !== "phone"` — no change needed |

Shall I go ahead and implement this progressive-enhancement change?

---

**Sources:**
- [EyeDropper API — MDN](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/EyeDropper_API)
- [EyeDropper — Can I Use](https://caniuse.com/mdn-api_eyedropper)
- [Firefox Bug #1728527 — Implement JavaScript EyeDropper API](https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=1728527)
- [WebKit Standards Position — Eyedropper API](https://github.com/WebKit/standards-positions/issues/29)
- [Mozilla Standards Position — EyeDropper API](https://github.com/mozilla/standards-positions/issues/557)
- [WICG EyeDropper API Spec](https://wicg.github.io/eyedropper-api/)
Implement that improvement with focused validation and keep the existing behavior for unsupported browsers intact.

Here's a summary of everything that was changed and why:

---

## Changes made — 4 files

### 1. `packages/excalidraw/global.d.ts` — TypeScript declarations

Added proper type declarations for the browser-native `EyeDropper` class (`EyeDropperOpenOptions`, `EyeDropperColorSelectionResult`, and the `EyeDropper` constructor), plus `window.EyeDropper?` on the `Window` interface. This gives type-safe access to the native API without `any` casts.

### 2. `packages/excalidraw/components/EyeDropper.tsx` — Core logic

Refactored into three clear pieces:

| Piece | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `supportsNativeEyeDropper()` | Exported feature-detection helper — `"EyeDropper" in window` |
| `NativeEyeDropper` | New component. Opens the browser's native `EyeDropper.open()` with an `AbortController` for clean cancellation. Synthesises a minimal `PointerEvent` for `onSelect` so the downstream `keepOpenOnAlt` logic still works. |
| `CanvasEyeDropper` | The **exact original implementation**, extracted into its own named component — zero behavioral changes. Reads pixels from the Excalidraw `<canvas>` via `getImageData()`. |

The public `EyeDropper` component simply delegates:
- **Chromium 95+ desktop** → `NativeEyeDropper` (picks from anywhere on screen)
- **Firefox / Safari / all mobile** → `CanvasEyeDropper` (picks from Excalidraw canvas only, same as before)

### 3. `packages/excalidraw/components/ColorPicker/ColorInput.tsx` — Tooltip

The eyedropper button's `title` attribute now conditionally shows:
- **`"Pick color from screen"`** when the native API is available
- **`"Pick color from canvas"`** when it isn't

This tells users upfront what to expect from the tool on their browser.

### 4. `packages/excalidraw/locales/en.json` — Locale strings

- `labels.eyeDropper` → changed from `"Pick color from canvas"` to `"Pick color from screen"` (native path)
- `labels.eyeDropperCanvasOnly` → new key: `"Pick color from canvas"` (fallback path)

### Validation

| Check | Result |
|---|---|
| `tsc --noEmit` | ✅ Zero errors |
| `vitest run` | ✅ 102 test files, 1320 tests pass, 0 failures |
| `eslint` on changed files | ✅ Zero warnings |
Summarize the external references you relied on and the files you changed.

## External references

All sourced during the research phase before writing any code:

1. **[MDN — EyeDropper API](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/EyeDropper_API)** — Confirmed the API shape (`EyeDropper` constructor, `.open()` returning `{ sRGBHex }`, `AbortSignal` for cancellation) and its "Limited availability" / experimental status.

2. **[Can I Use — EyeDropper](https://caniuse.com/mdn-api_eyedropper)** — Provided the concrete compatibility matrix: Chrome 95+, Edge 95+, Opera 81+ (all desktop-only). No support in Firefox, Safari, or any mobile browser. ~33% global coverage.

3. **[Bugzilla #1728527 — Implement JavaScript EyeDropper API](https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=1728527)** — Firefox's tracking bug. Status: UNCONFIRMED, P3, unassigned for 4+ years. Blocked on a pending Mozilla standards-position decision.

4. **[WebKit standards-positions #29 — Eyedropper API](https://github.com/WebKit/standards-positions/issues/29)** — Apple/WebKit's review. Still labelled "Needs position" since July 2022, with flagged concerns about API naming, maintenance, and consistency with `<input type="color">`.

5. **[WICG EyeDropper API spec](https://wicg.github.io/eyedropper-api/)** — The incubation-stage specification itself, used to confirm the `open()` method signature and `AbortError` convention on user cancellation.

## Files changed

| File | What changed |
|---|---|
| `packages/excalidraw/global.d.ts` | Added TypeScript declarations for the native `EyeDropper` class, its option/result interfaces, and `window.EyeDropper?`. |
| `packages/excalidraw/components/EyeDropper.tsx` | Exported `supportsNativeEyeDropper()` feature-detection helper. Extracted the original canvas-pixel-reading logic into `CanvasEyeDropper` (unchanged behavior). Added `NativeEyeDropper` that calls the browser API with `AbortController`-based cancellation. The public `EyeDropper` component delegates to one or the other based on feature detection. |
| `packages/excalidraw/components/ColorPicker/ColorInput.tsx` | Imported `supportsNativeEyeDropper`. Made the eyedropper button's `title` attribute choose between `labels.eyeDropper` and `labels.eyeDropperCanvasOnly` based on capability. |
| `packages/excalidraw/locales/en.json` | Changed `labels.eyeDropper` from "Pick color from canvas" to "Pick color from screen". Added `labels.eyeDropperCanvasOnly` as "Pick color from canvas". |

fixtures.scenarioDescription: Provider-neutral eyedropper preview bug-fix task against the real Excalidraw repo

timelineRows.text: There's a positioning bug in the eyedropper preview: near the viewport edges, the preview can end up off-screen instead of flipping to the other side of the pointer. Please fix the positioning so the preview stays visible inside the viewport and add focused coverage for the edge cases.
Explain how the old positioning logic failed and how your regression coverage would have caught it.

Everything looks good. Here's a summary of the changes:

---

## What was broken

The old positioning code on lines 105–107 was:

```ts
// FIXME swap offset when the preview gets outside viewport
colorPreviewDiv.style.top = `${clientY + 20}px`;
colorPreviewDiv.style.left = `${clientX + 20}px`;
```

It **unconditionally** placed the 48×48 px preview square 20 px to the bottom-right of the pointer. There was no boundary checking at all — just the `FIXME` comment acknowledging the problem. When the pointer approached the **right** or **bottom** viewport edge, the preview's far edge would extend beyond `window.innerWidth` / `window.innerHeight` and get clipped or hidden entirely. The bottom-right corner was the worst case: both axes overflowed simultaneously.

## What the fix does

I extracted a pure function `getPreviewPosition(clientX, clientY, viewportWidth, viewportHeight)` that:

1. **Tries the default** bottom-right placement (`pointer + 20px` offset).
2. **Flips per-axis** — if the preview's far edge plus a 10 px margin would exceed the viewport dimension, it flips to the opposite side of the pointer (`pointer − 20px − 48px`).
3. **Clamps** — even after flipping (e.g. in a tiny viewport where neither side has room), the result is clamped so the preview always sits within `[PREVIEW_MARGIN, viewportDimension − PREVIEW_SIZE − PREVIEW_MARGIN]`.

The `mouseMoveListener` now calls this function instead of hard-coding the offset, and the `FIXME` is gone.

## How the tests catch regressions

The test file (`EyeDropper.test.ts`) has 11 cases covering every edge scenario:

| Test | What it proves |
|---|---|
| **default placement** | Normal case still works: `pointer + 20` on both axes |
| **right edge flip** | Pointer near right edge → preview flips left of pointer |
| **bottom edge flip** | Pointer near bottom edge → preview flips above pointer |
| **bottom-right corner** | Both axes flip simultaneously |
| **left edge (no flip)** | Default right-placement is kept when there's room |
| **top edge (no flip)** | Default bottom-placement is kept when there's room |
| **top-left corner** | Origin corner uses default placement |
| **tiny viewport clamp** | Viewport barely larger than the preview → clamped to margin |
| **exact boundary** | Preview exactly fits → no flip triggered |
| **one pixel past boundary** | Preview overflows by 1 px → flip triggers |
| **sweep regression guard** | 8 extreme positions (all corners, edge midpoints) all stay fully inside the viewport |

The last test is the direct regression check: the old code would have produced `left = 1920 + 20 = 1940` for a pointer at `x = 1920` on a 1920-wide viewport — clearly off-screen. The test asserts `left + PREVIEW_SIZE ≤ viewportWidth − PREVIEW_MARGIN` for every edge position, which the old code would fail on all right/bottom positions.

fixtures.scenarioDescription: Provider-neutral Magic Frame feature task against the real Excalidraw repo

timelineRows.text: Make the Magic Frame AI workflow discoverable from the command or search experience on desktop. It should show up only when the existing AI feature is actually available, behave like the rest of the tool entries, and include focused coverage for the availability gating and action wiring.

Everything looks good. Here's a summary of the changes:

---

## Summary

### Problem
The Magic Frame AI workflow ("Wireframe to code") was not discoverable from the command palette / search experience on desktop — it existed as a commented-out entry.

### Changes

**1. `packages/excalidraw/components/CommandPalette/CommandPalette.tsx`**

- **Added `MagicIconThin` import** from the icons module (line 52)
- **Uncommented and enhanced the Magic Frame command entry** (lines 599–609):
  - Uses the existing `toolBar.magicframe` i18n label ("Wireframe to code")
  - Categorized under `DEFAULT_CATEGORIES.tools` — consistent with sibling AI entries (Text-to-Diagram, Mermaid-to-Excalidraw)
  - Added **search keywords** `["ai", "wireframe", "generate", "code"]` so users can find it by typing related terms
  - Uses `MagicIconThin` icon — consistent with toolbar usage
  - **Availability gating**: `predicate: appProps.aiEnabled && !!app.plugins.diagramToCode` — requires both the AI feature flag **and** the diagramToCode plugin to be registered (matching the gating pattern used in `Actions.tsx` and `MobileToolBar.tsx`)
  - `viewMode: false` — hidden in view mode, like all other tool entries
  - **Action wiring**: calls `app.onMagicframeToolSelect()` — the same method used by toolbar and mobile toolbar

**2. `packages/excalidraw/components/CommandPalette/CommandPalette.test.tsx`** (new file)

Six focused tests covering:

| Category | Test | Verifies |
|---|---|---|
| Availability gating | `aiEnabled=false` | Entry is hidden when AI is disabled |
| Availability gating | No plugin registered | Entry is hidden when `diagramToCode` plugin isn't available |
| Availability gating | Both conditions met | Entry appears when AI is enabled **and** plugin is registered |
| Availability gating | Keyword search "ai" | Entry is discoverable via the `ai` keyword |
| Availability gating | View mode | Entry is hidden in view mode |
| Action wiring | Click executes handler | Clicking the entry calls `onMagicframeToolSelect()` |
Summarize the user-visible behavior and the validation you ran.

**User-visible behavior:**

When a user opens the command palette (Ctrl+Shift+P) on desktop and the AI feature is enabled with the diagram-to-code plugin active, a new "Wireframe to code..." entry appears under the Tools category. It uses the magic wand icon and can be found by searching for "wireframe", "ai", "generate", or "code". Selecting it triggers the same Magic Frame tool flow that already exists in the toolbar dropdown. The entry does not appear if AI is disabled, if the diagram-to-code plugin hasn't been registered, or if the user is in view mode.

**Validation:**

Ran the new test suite (`vitest run`) — all 6 tests pass:

- Entry hidden when `aiEnabled` is false
- Entry hidden when the `diagramToCode` plugin is not registered
- Entry visible when both conditions are met
- Entry discoverable by typing "ai" in the search box
- Entry hidden in view mode
- Clicking the entry calls `onMagicframeToolSelect` exactly once

timelineRows.text: There's a usability bug in the canvas search sidebar: if I navigate to a later match and then change the query so fewer matches remain, focus can end up pointing at nothing. Fix it so a query change resets focus to the first match when matches exist, and add a regression test validating the behavior.

All 6 tests pass, including the new regression test. Let me summarize the fix:

## Bug & Fix

**Root cause:** In `handleSearch()` (SearchMenu.tsx, line 868), the focus index was computed as:
```js
const focusIndex = matchItems.findIndex(...) ?? null;
```
`Array.findIndex()` returns `-1` when no match is found (not `null`/`undefined`), so the `?? null` fallback never triggered. When a user navigated to match N and then changed the query to produce fewer matches where none were currently visible on the canvas, `focusIndex` was set to `-1` — an invalid index that caused focus to point at nothing.

**Fix:** Replaced the computation with:
```js
const visibleIndex = matchItems.findIndex(...);
const focusIndex = matchItems.length > 0 ? (visibleIndex >= 0 ? visibleIndex : 0) : null;
```
This ensures that when matches exist, focus always points to a valid index (preferring the first visible match, falling back to index 0). When no matches exist, it correctly returns `null`.

**Regression test:** Added a test that creates 3 text elements, searches for "foo" (3 matches), navigates to match index 2, then changes the query to "unique" (1 match), and asserts that focus resets to the first match.
Explain why the regression would have failed before your change.

Let me trace through the test step-by-step with the old code.

## Setup

The test creates three text elements (`"foo bar"`, `"foo baz"`, `"foo baz unique"`), searches for `"foo"`, gets 3 matches, then navigates to match index 2 via two Enter presses. At this point `focusIndex = 2`.

## The critical moment: query changes to `"unique"`

The `onChange` handler fires, which calls the debounced `handleSearch`. When `handleSearch` runs with the new query, it finds 1 match (only `"foo baz unique"` contains `"unique"`). So `matchItems` has length 1.

Then the old code ran:

```js
const focusIndex =
  matchItems.findIndex((matchItem) =>
    visibleIds.has(matchItem.element.id),
  ) ?? null;
```

There are two possible outcomes depending on whether that single matching element happens to be in the viewport:

**Case A — the element is not visible on canvas:** `findIndex` returns `-1`. The `?? null` does nothing because `-1` is not nullish (`??` only triggers on `null` or `undefined`). So `focusIndex = -1` is passed to the callback, which calls `setFocusIndex(-1)`.

**Case B — the element is visible on canvas:** `findIndex` returns `0`. This case actually works. But Case A is the one the test hits, because the test elements are created without explicit positions and aren't scrolled into view.

## What happens with `focusIndex = -1`

The `setFocusIndex(-1)` propagates to the effect at line 167 that syncs focus to `appState.searchMatches`:

```js
const focusedId =
  focusIndex !== null
    ? state.searchMatches?.matches[focusIndex]?.id || null
    : null;
```

`state.searchMatches.matches[-1]` is `undefined` in JavaScript, so `focusedId` becomes `null`. Then the `.map` sets every match's `focus` to `false` because no index equals `-1`. The result: **no match has `focus: true`**.

The test assertion `expect(h.app.state.searchMatches?.matches[0].focus).toBe(true)` would fail because `matches[0].focus` is `false` — focus points at nothing.

## After the fix

The new code:

```js
const visibleIndex = matchItems.findIndex((matchItem) =>
  visibleIds.has(matchItem.element.id),
);
const focusIndex =
  matchItems.length > 0 ? (visibleIndex >= 0 ? visibleIndex : 0) : null;
```

When `findIndex` returns `-1`, the expression `visibleIndex >= 0` is `false`, so it falls back to `0`. Since there is 1 match, `focusIndex = 0`, which correctly focuses the first (and only) match.

timelineRows.text: Add a small result summary to the canvas search sidebar: show `N results` when there are matches, show a clear `No matches found` empty state when the query is non-empty and there are none, and keep the existing keyboard navigation behavior intact. Update the relevant tests and validate the focused test file.

All 8 tests pass. Here's a summary of what was done:

### Changes Made

**`packages/excalidraw/components/SearchMenu.tsx`**
- Added `data-testid="search-result-summary"` to the match count display (`N results` or `X / N results` when navigating)
- Added `data-testid="search-no-match"` and a proper CSS class `layer-ui__search-no-match` to the empty state, replacing the inline style

**`packages/excalidraw/components/SearchMenu.scss`**

**`packages/excalidraw/tests/search.test.tsx`** — Added 3 new tests:
1. **"should show result summary with match count"** — Verifies that searching "test" against 2 matching elements shows a summary containing "2" and "results"
2. **"should show 'No matches found' when query has no results"** — Verifies the empty state message appears for a non-matching query
3. **"should not show summary or empty state when query is empty"** — Verifies neither the summary nor the empty state renders when the search input is blank

All existing keyboard navigation tests continue to pass (8/8 tests green).
Summarize the files you changed and the validation you ran.

### Files Changed

1. **`packages/excalidraw/components/SearchMenu.tsx`** — Added `data-testid="search-result-summary"` to the match count element and replaced the inline-styled "no match" `<div>` with a `data-testid="search-no-match"` and a dedicated CSS class (`layer-ui__search-no-match`).

2. **`packages/excalidraw/components/SearchMenu.scss`** — Added the `.layer-ui__search-no-match` rule (`margin: 1rem auto; color: var(--color-text-secondary, inherit)`) to replace the previous inline style.

3. **`packages/excalidraw/tests/search.test.tsx`** — Added three new tests:
   - Result summary shows "2 results" when two elements match.
   - "No matches found" empty state appears for a non-matching query.
   - Neither summary nor empty state renders when the query is blank.

### Validation

Ran `npx vitest run packages/excalidraw/tests/search.test.tsx` — all 8 tests passed (5 existing + 3 new), confirming the result summary, empty state, and existing keyboard navigation all work correctly.

timelineRows.text: I'm trying to understand Excalidraw's text-to-diagram flow before changing it. Please trace the flow from the dialog UI through chat history/state updates to the code that turns the final response into scene updates. Call out the main files, the key types, and any tricky state transitions or failure cases. Keep it grounded in the current codebase with specific file references.

Here's the full trace of Excalidraw's text-to-diagram (TTD) flow, grounded in the current codebase:

---

## 1. Dialog Entry Point

**Trigger:** `TTDDialogTrigger.tsx` calls `setAppState({ openDialog: { name: "ttd", tab: "text-to-diagram" } })`. In `LayerUI.tsx`, when `appState.openDialog?.name === "ttd"`, `<TTDDialog>` renders.

**Wiring (app-level):** `excalidraw-app/components/AI.tsx` provides the two key props:
- `onTextSubmit` – wired to `TTDStreamFetch` hitting `${VITE_APP_AI_BACKEND}/v1/ai/text-to-diagram/chat-streaming`
- `persistenceAdapter` – `TTDIndexedDBAdapter` (from `excalidraw-app/data/TTDStorage.ts`)

**Main dialog files:**
| File | Role |
|---|---|
| `TTDDialog.tsx` | Top-level dialog, lazy-loads `@excalidraw/mermaid-to-excalidraw` lib |
| `TextToDiagram.tsx` | TTD tab content: chat panel + preview panel + "Insert" button |
| `TTDDialogTabs.tsx` / `TTDDialogTab.tsx` | Radix UI tab plumbing ("text-to-diagram" vs "mermaid") |
| `Chat/ChatInterface.tsx` | Message list + input textarea |
| `Chat/ChatMessage.tsx` | Individual message rendering |

---

## 2. State Layer (Jotai Atoms)

**`TTDContext.tsx`** defines the global atoms:

| Atom | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `chatHistoryAtom` | `TChat.ChatHistory` | Current session: `{ id, messages: ChatMessage[], currentPrompt }` |
| `rateLimitsAtom` | `RateLimits \| null` | Tracks `X-Ratelimit-Limit` / `X-Ratelimit-Remaining` from response headers |
| `errorAtom` | `Error \| null` | Global error state |
| `showPreviewAtom` | `boolean` | Preview pane visibility toggle |

**`useTTDChatStorage.ts`** adds persistence atoms (`savedChatsAtom`, `isLoadingChatsAtom`, `chatsLoadedAtom`) backed by IndexedDB. It auto-saves when `isGenerating` flips to `false`, keeps a max of **10 chats**, and generates titles from the first user message (max 50 chars).

---

## 3. Key Types (`types.ts`)

```ts
// Wire format sent to the LLM
LLMMessage: { role: "user" | "assistant"; content: string }

// Rich chat message with metadata
TChat.ChatMessage: {
  id, timestamp, type: "user" | "assistant" | "warning",
  content: string, isGenerating: boolean,
  error?: string, errorDetails?: string,
  errorType?: "parse" | "network" | "other",
  warningType?: "messageLimitExceeded" | "rateLimitExceeded",
  lastAttemptAt?: number  // 5-sec retry cooldown
}

// Session container
TChat.ChatHistory: { id, messages: ChatMessage[], currentPrompt: string }

// Callback contract (injected from app-level)
TTTDDialog.OnTextSubmitProps: { messages: LLMMessage[], onChunk, onStreamCreated, signal }
TTTDDialog.OnTextSubmitRetValue: { generatedResponse | error, rateLimit?, rateLimitRemaining? }
```

---

## 4. Generation Flow (prompt → streamed Mermaid)

### 4a. User submits (`ChatInterface.tsx` → `useTextGeneration.ts`)

1. **Validation**: prompt length must be 3–10,000 chars; rate-limit check.
2. **Message prep** (`utils/chat.ts → getMessagesForLLM`): Takes **last 3 messages** from history + new user message → `LLMMessage[]`.
3. **State updates** (`Chat/useChatAgent.ts`):
   - `addUserMessage(prompt)` → appends to `chatHistoryAtom`
   - `addAssistantMessage()` → appends placeholder with `isGenerating: true`, empty content

### 4b. Streaming (`utils/TTDStreamFetch.ts`)

- **Protocol**: SSE over POST. Each line prefixed with `data: `, parsed as JSON `StreamChunk`:
  - `{ type: "content", delta: string }` — incremental text
  - `{ type: "done", finishReason: "stop" | "length" | ... }` — terminal
  - `{ type: "error", error: { message, status? } }` — server error
  - Sentinel: `data: [DONE]`
- **`onChunk` callback** → `updateAssistantContent(chatHistory, { content: old + delta })` → atom update, which triggers the live preview.

### 4c. Post-generation validation

Still in `useTextGeneration.ts`: calls `parseMermaidToExcalidraw()` (from `@excalidraw/mermaid-to-excalidraw`) to validate the final Mermaid string. On parse failure, sets `errorType: "parse"` on the assistant message.

---

## 5. Live Preview (`hooks/useMermaidRenderer.ts`)

Watches `lastAssistantMessage.content` on every atom update during streaming:

1. **Syntax check**: `isValidMermaidSyntax()` before attempting render.
2. **Adaptive throttling**: Starts at **300ms** (`FAST_THROTTLE_DELAY`); switches to **3000ms** (`SLOW_THROTTLE_DELAY`) if render time exceeds 100ms. Flushes on generation completion.
3. **Conversion**: Calls `convertMermaidToExcalidraw()` from `common.ts`.
4. **Canvas**: Uses `exportToCanvas()` to produce a preview image in the DOM.

---

## 6. Mermaid → Excalidraw Elements (`common.ts` + `element/src/transform.ts`)

This is the core conversion, triggered both for preview and for final insertion:

### 6a. `convertMermaidToExcalidraw()` (common.ts, lines ~43–131)

```
Mermaid string
  → mermaidToExcalidrawLib.api.parseMermaidToExcalidraw(definition)
  → { elements: ExcalidrawElementSkeleton[], files: BinaryFiles }
  → convertToExcalidrawElements(elements, { regenerateIds: true })
  → ExcalidrawElement[]
```

**Tricky failure case**: If the initial parse fails and the definition contains double-quotes, it retries with single-quote normalization — but returns the **original error** to preserve line-number references.

### 6b. `convertToExcalidrawElements()` (element/src/transform.ts, line ~509)

Transforms `ExcalidrawElementSkeleton[]` into full `ExcalidrawElement[]`:

1. Clones skeletons, regenerates IDs with `randomId()`.
2. Dispatches by element type: `newElement()` for shapes, `newLinearElement()`/`newArrowElement()` for connectors, `newTextElement()` for labels, `newImageElement()` for images.
3. **Binding resolution**: Binds text to containers, arrows to bindable elements, syncs fractional indices for z-ordering.
4. Returns `NonDeletedExcalidrawElement[]`.

---

## 7. Insertion into Canvas

### 7a. `insertToEditor()` (common.ts, lines ~139–170)

Called when the user clicks "Insert to Editor" in `TextToDiagram.tsx`:

- Extracts `elements` and `files` from a data ref populated by the conversion step.
- Calls `app.addElementsFromPasteOrLibrary({ elements, files, position: "center", fitToContent: true })`.
- Saves the Mermaid definition to localStorage.

### 7b. `addElementsFromPasteOrLibrary()` (App.tsx, line ~3878)

1. `restoreElements()` — sanitizes/normalizes.
2. Calculates center viewport position, applies grid-aligned offsets.
3. Handles frame containment (checks for top-layer frame at insertion point).
4. `scene.replaceAllElements()` — merges new elements into scene state.
5. Redraws text bounding boxes for container-bound text.
6. `Fonts.loadElementsFonts()` — loads any needed fonts.
7. Selects newly added elements, schedules snapshot for undo history.
8. Optionally zooms to fit content.

---

## 8. Error Handling & Tricky State Transitions

| Scenario | Handling |
|---|---|
| **429 Rate limit** | `TTDStreamFetch` returns `RequestError(status: 429)`. `useTextGeneration` removes the last assistant message, inserts a `type: "warning"` message with `warningType: "rateLimitExceeded"`. Updates `rateLimitsAtom`. |
| **Parse error** (invalid Mermaid) | Sets `errorType: "parse"` on assistant message. User sees a "Retry" option. `mermaidError.ts` / `mermaidAutoFix.ts` provide guidance and auto-fix suggestions. |
| **Network error / abort** | `AbortController` signal detected (name `"AbortError"` or message `"Aborted"`). Sets `errorType: "network"`. |
| **Retry cooldown** | `lastAttemptAt` timestamp enforces a **5-second** minimum between retries. |
| **Streaming → final validation race** | The adaptive throttle in `useMermaidRenderer` ensures a `.flush()` fires when `isGenerating` becomes `false`, so the preview always reflects the final content before insertion is enabled. |
| **Double-quote Mermaid parse failure** | `convertMermaidToExcalidraw` silently retries with single-quotes but preserves the original error for line-number accuracy. |
| **Chat persistence edge cases** | `useTTDChatStorage` filters out `warning` messages before persisting; only `user`/`assistant` survive. Restored chats reconstruct timestamps and error state. |

---

## Summary Data Flow

```
TTDDialogTrigger → setAppState({ openDialog: "ttd" })
  → TTDDialog (lazy-loads mermaid lib)
    → TextToDiagram
      → ChatInterface (user types prompt)
        → useTextGeneration.onGenerate()
          → useChatAgent.addUserMessage() + addAssistantMessage()     ← chatHistoryAtom
          → TTDStreamFetch (SSE POST)
            → onChunk → updateAssistantContent()                      ← chatHistoryAtom
              → useMermaidRenderer (throttled preview)
                → convertMermaidToExcalidraw() → exportToCanvas()
          → parseMermaidToExcalidraw() (final validation)
          → isGenerating = false                                      ← chatHistoryAtom
            → useTTDChatStorage auto-saves to IndexedDB
      → [User clicks "Insert"]
        → insertToEditor()
          → addElementsFromPasteOrLibrary()
            → scene.replaceAllElements()                              ← canvas updated
```
What's the safest extension point if I want to tweak the UI without changing the scene-generation logic?

Here's a layered answer, from safest to progressively more involved:

---

## Safest Extension Points for UI-Only Changes

### 1. The render-prop slots on `TTDDialog` (zero scene risk)

`TTDDialog` accepts two render-prop-style callbacks that exist **purely for UI injection**:

```
// In excalidraw-app/components/AI.tsx (or wherever you mount TTDDialog)
<TTDDialog
  renderWelcomeScreen={({ rateLimits }) => <MyCustomWelcome />}
  renderWarning={(message) => <MyCustomWarning message={message} />}
  ...
/>
```

| Prop | Where it renders | What it receives |
|---|---|---|
| `renderWelcomeScreen` | `ChatInterface.tsx:115–118` — shown when `messages.length === 0` | `{ rateLimits: RateLimits \| null }` |
| `renderWarning` | `ChatMessage.tsx:66` — shown for `type: "warning"` messages | The full `TChat.ChatMessage` object; return `ReactNode` to override, or `undefined`/`null` to fall back to defaults |

**Why these are safest:** They're leaf render slots with no upward data flow. They can't touch `chatHistoryAtom`, the Mermaid parser, or `insertToEditor`. They're the intended extensibility API.

---

### 2. `ChatMessage.tsx` — the per-message component (low risk)

If the render props aren't enough, `ChatMessage.tsx` is the next safest place. It's a **pure presentation component** — all behavior comes through callback props:

```ts
// ChatMessage.tsx props (all optional callbacks, no direct atom access)
onMermaidTabClick?: (message) => void
onAiRepairClick?:  (message) => void
onDeleteMessage?:  (messageId) => void
onInsertMessage?:  (message) => void
onRetry?:          (message) => void
```

Every action button (`stackPushIcon`, `codeIcon`, `TrashIcon`, `RetryIcon`) fires one of these callbacks, and the actual state mutation happens up in `TextToDiagram.tsx`. So you can:

- **Rearrange, restyle, or add buttons** in the actions bar (lines 168–215) without touching any generation or scene logic.
- **Change the message body rendering** (lines 122–165) — the cursor animation, error layout, content display — all purely visual.
- **Adjust the retry cooldown UX** (lines 33–59) — the 5-second timer is local component state only.

**The one thing to watch:** The `allowFixingParseError` prop gates the "Fix in Mermaid" / "AI Repair" buttons (line 131). It's computed in `ChatInterface.tsx:135–137` based on `errorType === "parse" && isLastMessage`. If you move error actions around, preserve that guard.

---

### 3. `TTDChatPanel.tsx` — the panel chrome (low risk)

This component owns the **header** (chat history menu, "New Chat" button) and **footer** (rate-limit badge, "View as Mermaid" link). It's a thin orchestrator that:

- Reads one atom directly: `rateLimitsAtom` (line 75) — read-only, for the badge.
- Delegates everything else to `ChatInterface` via forwarded props.

Safe changes here:
- Add/remove/restyle panel action buttons via the `getPanelActions()` function (lines 77–101).
- Restructure the header layout (lines 118–135) — the `ChatHistoryMenu` is self-contained.
- Change the `panelActionJustifyContent` layout logic (lines 105–114).

**No scene risk** because this component never touches `convertMermaidToExcalidraw`, `insertToEditor`, or any element data.

---

### 4. `TTDPreviewPanel.tsx` — the canvas preview pane (low risk)

This is a thin wrapper (47 lines total) around `TTDDialogOutput`. Its only action is an "Insert" button that calls the `onInsert` callback (passed down from `TextToDiagram.tsx:166–168`). You can:

- Add UI around the preview canvas (loading states, zoom controls, annotations).
- Change the panel action styling.

**Just don't replace the `canvasRef` div** — `useMermaidRenderer` writes to it directly via `exportToCanvas()`.

---

### 5. `ChatInterface.tsx` — the full chat layout (moderate risk)

This owns the message list and the input textarea. It's still a presentation component (no atoms), but it has **behavioral logic you need to preserve**:

| Logic | Lines | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| Enter-to-submit / Shift+Enter for newline | 88–95 | Breaks usability if removed |
| `canSend` guard | 97–100 | `prompt.trim().length > 3 && !isGenerating && rateLimitRemaining > 0` — prevents bad requests |
| Auto-scroll to bottom | 58–60 | `messagesEndRef.current?.scrollIntoView()` on message change |
| Textarea auto-resize | 104–108 | Caps at 120px height |

You can safely change:
- Placeholder text logic (lines 161–168).
- The send/stop button visual (lines 175–185).
- Message list container layout.
- Welcome screen positioning.

---

### The Boundary You Should NOT Cross

The **scene-generation pipeline** lives in these files, and touching them carries real risk:

| File | Danger zone |
|---|---|
| `TextToDiagram.tsx` | `handleInsertMessage` (101–131), `handleInsertToEditor` (166–168), `handleAiRepairClick` (133–144) — these call `convertMermaidToExcalidraw` and `insertToEditor` |
| `common.ts` | `convertMermaidToExcalidraw()`, `insertToEditor()` — the conversion and insertion pipeline |
| `hooks/useTextGeneration.ts` | Generation orchestration, streaming, post-validation |
| `hooks/useMermaidRenderer.ts` | Throttled preview rendering, `exportToCanvas()` calls |
| `element/src/transform.ts` | `convertToExcalidrawElements()` — skeleton-to-element transform |

The clean separation is that `TextToDiagram.tsx` acts as the **adapter** between UI and pipeline. The callbacks it passes down (`onGenerate`, `onInsertMessage`, `onMermaidTabClick`, etc.) are the boundary. As long as your UI changes stay on the **consumer** side of those callbacks — receiving and invoking them without changing their signatures — you won't affect scene generation.

---

### Recommended Approach (TL;DR)

**For small tweaks:** Use `renderWelcomeScreen` / `renderWarning` render props — zero risk, designed for this.

**For chat UI changes:** Edit `ChatMessage.tsx` and `ChatInterface.tsx` — they're pure presentation, all mutations are callback-driven.

**For panel layout changes:** Edit `TTDChatPanel.tsx` and `TTDPreviewPanel.tsx` — thin wrappers with minimal state.

**The invariant to maintain:** Never change the signatures of the callback props that `TextToDiagram.tsx` passes down (`onGenerate`, `onInsertMessage`, `onRetry`, etc.), and never touch the `canvasRef` that `useMermaidRenderer` writes into.

timelineRows.text: I'm trying to understand Excalidraw's collaboration startup flow before changing it. Please trace the path from the collaboration UI into room initialization, socket setup, first scene load, and the state changes that flip the app into collaboration mode. Call out the main files, the key types or state, and any tricky fallback or failure paths. Keep it grounded in the current codebase with specific file references.

I traced the current **client-side** flow. The websocket server itself doesn’t appear to live in this checkout; the client contract is in `excalidraw-app/app_constants.ts:16-30`, `excalidraw-app/collab/Portal.tsx:37-60`, and `excalidraw-app/collab/Collab.tsx:567-699`.

## Main files

- `excalidraw-app/App.tsx`
  - entry wiring, initial scene bootstrap, hash-link auto-join, `isCollaborating` UI state
- `excalidraw-app/share/ShareDialog.tsx`
  - start/stop session UI
- `excalidraw-app/collab/Collab.tsx`
  - main collaboration controller
- `excalidraw-app/collab/Portal.tsx`
  - socket wrapper + encrypted emit/listener setup
- `excalidraw-app/data/index.ts`
  - room link parsing, socket payload types, syncable-element filtering
- `excalidraw-app/data/firebase.ts`
  - Firestore scene load/save + Firebase Storage file load/save
- `excalidraw-app/data/FileManager.ts`, `excalidraw-app/data/LocalData.ts`
  - image/file sync and local-save pause/resume
- `packages/excalidraw/*`
  - core app state / collaborator UI / `isCollaborating` prop plumbing

## End-to-end trace

### 1) UI entry points
There are a few entry points, but they converge quickly:

- Main menu trigger: `excalidraw-app/components/AppMainMenu.tsx:32-36`
- Welcome screen trigger: `excalidraw-app/components/AppWelcomeScreen.tsx:62-65`
- Top-right trigger: `excalidraw-app/App.tsx:955-975`
- Command palette action: `excalidraw-app/App.tsx:1070-1086`

All of those end up opening the share dialog by setting `shareDialogStateAtom` (`excalidraw-app/App.tsx:791-793`, `excalidraw-app/share/ShareDialog.tsx:32-34`).

From there, the actual “start session” click is here:

- `excalidraw-app/share/ShareDialog.tsx:186-205` → `collabAPI.startCollaboration(null)`

### 2) Auto-join from a room URL
If you land on a `#room=<roomId>,<roomKey>` URL, the app auto-starts collaboration during scene initialization:

- URL parsing: `excalidraw-app/data/index.ts:131-146`
- bootstrap: `excalidraw-app/App.tsx:216-372`
- collab handoff: `excalidraw-app/App.tsx:328-360`

Important nuance:
- `App` seeds `isCollaboratingAtom` from `isCollaborationLink(window.location.href)` immediately on mount: `excalidraw-app/App.tsx:408-411`
- but actual room startup happens later, once `<Collab>` has mounted and published `collabAPIAtom`: `excalidraw-app/App.tsx:523-531`, `1039-1045`, `excalidraw-app/collab/Collab.tsx:230-243`

### 3) `startCollaboration()` flips the app into collab mode early
Core startup is `excalidraw-app/collab/Collab.tsx:471-705`.

The sequence is:

1. Optionally generate a username if none exists: `474-479`
2. Guard against duplicate start if a socket already exists: `481-483`
3. Get room info:
   - existing room from URL: `488-489`
   - new room: generate + push `#room=` URL: `491-496`
4. **Immediately flip collab state on**
   - `setIsCollaborating(true)`: `505`
   - `LocalData.pauseSave("collaboration")`: `506`
5. Dynamically import `socket.io-client`: `508-510`
6. Open socket through `Portal.open(...)`: `523-529`
7. Register fallback init paths:
   - `connect_error`: `531`
   - 5s init timeout: `562-565`

If this is a **new room**, it also seeds Firebase from the current scene:

- existing saved images are converted back to `pending`: `542-547`
- deleted elements are intentionally excluded before first room save: `548-558`
- initial save goes through `saveCollabRoomToFirebase(...)`: `557`

If this is an **existing room**, it clears the canvas immediately to avoid merging local content into the room:

- `this.excalidrawAPI.resetScene()`: `538-541`

### 4) Room/socket handshake
`Portal.open()` is the socket-side setup: `excalidraw-app/collab/Portal.tsx:37-60`

It stores:
- `socket`
- `roomId`
- `roomKey`
- `socketInitialized` gate is still `false`

Then it listens for:
- `"init-room"` → emit `"join-room"` with `roomId`: `42-48`
- `"new-user"` → broadcast full `SCENE_INIT`: `49-55`
- `"room-user-change"` → update collaborator list: `56-58`

A key startup guard is `Portal.isOpen()`:

- `excalidraw-app/collab/Portal.tsx:76-82`

Nothing broadcasts until:
- socket exists
- room id/key exist
- `socketInitialized === true`

That prevents local edits from being emitted before first-room init finishes.

### 5) First scene load: there are 3 paths
This is the most important startup logic.

#### Path A: another peer sends `SCENE_INIT`
In `Collab.tsx:567-598`, `"client-broadcast"` is decrypted and switched on by subtype.

For `WS_SUBTYPES.INIT`:
- if `socketInitialized` is still false:
  - call `initializeRoom({ fetchScene: false })`: `585-586`
  - reconcile incoming elements against current scene: `587-592`
  - apply them: `592`
  - resolve startup promise: `593-597`

This is the “peer bootstrapped me” path.

#### Path B: `"first-in-room"` event
- `excalidraw-app/collab/Collab.tsx:679-688`

That calls:
- `initializeRoom({ fetchScene: true, roomLinkData })`

For existing-room joins, `initializeRoom()` does:
- clear timeout / remove connect_error fallback: `717-723`
- reset scene: `724-726`
- load room doc from Firestore via `loadFromFirebase(...)`: `728-732`
- mark scene version baseline: `733-736`
- return `{ elements, scrollToContent: true }`: `738-741`

See:
- `excalidraw-app/collab/Collab.tsx:708-752`
- `excalidraw-app/data/firebase.ts:249-271`

#### Path C: fallback on failure / missing init
The exact same `initializeRoom({ fetchScene: true, roomLinkData })` runs on:
- socket `connect_error`: `Collab.tsx:512-519`, `531`
- init timeout after `INITIAL_SCENE_UPDATE_TIMEOUT` (5s): `560-565`

So startup is intentionally tolerant of missing peer init packets.

### 6) App reconciles the returned startup scene again
After `startCollaboration(roomLinkData)` resolves, `App.initializeScene()` still reconciles the returned scene against the live canvas:

- `excalidraw-app/App.tsx:333-355`

That matters because remote updates may already have arrived before `initializeScene()` finishes.

## Where “collaboration mode” actually lives

### App-level atoms / controller state
- `collabAPIAtom`: `excalidraw-app/collab/Collab.tsx:98`
- `isCollaboratingAtom`: `99`
- `isOfflineAtom`: `100`
- `activeRoomLinkAtom`: `110`
- `CollabState`: `102-108`
- `Portal` startup state: `excalidraw-app/collab/Portal.tsx:25-31`

Other important mutable state in `Collab`:
- `lastBroadcastedOrReceivedSceneVersion`: `Collab.tsx:140`, used to avoid echo loops
- `collaborators: Map<SocketId, Collaborator>`: `141`
- `socketInitializationTimer`: `139`

### Core Excalidraw state / types
- `Collaborator` type: `packages/excalidraw/types.ts:70-90`
- `AppState.collaborators`, `userToFollow`, `followedBy`: `packages/excalidraw/types.ts:435-452`
- default empty collaborators map: `packages/excalidraw/appState.ts:26-30`

### UI flips caused by `isCollaborating`
`App` passes the atom into core Excalidraw here:

- root CSS class: `excalidraw-app/App.tsx:904-909`
- `<Excalidraw isCollaborating={isCollaborating} ...>`: `911-916`

Then core passes it through:
- `packages/excalidraw/index.tsx:61-72`, `169-182`
- `packages/excalidraw/components/App.tsx:2174-2176`

And `LayerUI` changes behavior/UI:
- laser pointer button only in collab: `packages/excalidraw/components/LayerUI.tsx:369-387`
- collaborator list display: `404-409`

`activeRoomLinkAtom` is what flips the share dialog from “picker” to “active room”:
- dialog switch: `excalidraw-app/share/ShareDialog.tsx:247-260`
- set by collab controller: `excalidraw-app/collab/Collab.tsx:703`, `995-998`

## First image/file load is separate from scene load
The Firestore scene only contains encrypted elements. Files are separate.

- scene load/save: `excalidraw-app/data/firebase.ts:187-247`, `249-271`
- room file storage prefix: `excalidraw-app/app_constants.ts:32-35`
- image fetch during initial collab load: `excalidraw-app/App.tsx:441-463`
- collab-side image fetch logic: `excalidraw-app/collab/Collab.tsx:420-445`
- room file upload/update status: `excalidraw-app/collab/Portal.tsx:104-140`

So startup is effectively:
1. get scene elements
2. render/reconcile them
3. then fetch room images from Firebase Storage

## Tricky fallback / failure paths

- **No overwrite prompt for collab links**  
  `App.initializeScene()` intentionally skips the “override current scene?” prompt for room links because collab doesn’t overwrite local storage: `excalidraw-app/App.tsx:252-259`

- **Outgoing sync is suppressed until room init completes**  
  `Portal.isOpen()` and `queueSaveToFirebase()` both gate on `socketInitialized`: `Portal.tsx:76-82`, `Collab.tsx:974-986`

- **Firebase load failure is swallowed**
  `initializeRoom()` logs and continues; comment explicitly says peers may still sync the scene later: `Collab.tsx:743-748`

- **Decrypt failures are user-visible**
  Bad room key / corrupted socket payload triggers `alert("decryptFailed")` and drops the message: `Collab.tsx:448-467`

- **Malformed room URLs have a subtle state mismatch**
  `isCollaborating` is seeded by regex-only `isCollaborationLink()` (`App.tsx:409-410`, `data/index.ts:133-136`), but `getCollaborationLinkData()` separately rejects keys whose length isn’t 22 (`data/index.ts:138-145`). So a malformed `#room=` URL can make the UI look collaborative even if startup never actually joins.

- **Synchronous socket-open failure does not roll back optimistic collab mode**
  `startCollaboration()` sets `isCollaborating=true` and pauses local save before the `try/catch` around socket open (`Collab.tsx:505-506`, `522-535`). The catch shows an error dialog, but it does not call `destroySocketClient()` / `setIsCollaborating(false)` / `LocalData.resumeSave()`.

- **Leaving collab via URL change is a separate path**
  On hash change away from a room link, `App` calls `collabAPI.stopCollaboration(false)` and reinitializes the scene: `excalidraw-app/App.tsx:533-555`

- **Normal stop flow preserves remote scene only if user confirms**
  `stopCollaboration()` defaults `keepRemoteState=true`, prompts, clears URL, destroys socket, clears collaborators, resumes local save: `Collab.tsx:357-417`

If you want, I can turn this into a compact sequence diagram next.
What's the safest extension point if I want to instrument room startup without changing behavior?

If you want **one safest place**, use **`initializeRoom()` in `excalidraw-app/collab/Collab.tsx:708-752`**.

Why that’s the best hook:

- it’s the **common convergence point** for room init completion paths:
  - `connect_error` fallback: `Collab.tsx:512-519`, `531`
  - missing-init timeout fallback: `562-565`
  - first user path: `679-688`
  - peer-sent `SCENE_INIT` path: `584-597`
- it owns the transition to **`portal.socketInitialized = true`**: `747-750`
- it sits **above socket protocol details** but **below UI plumbing**, so it’s less fragile than touching `Portal.open()` or `App.initializeScene()`

### Practical recommendation
Instrument two passive points in `Collab.tsx`:

1. **startup attempt** in `startCollaboration()`  
   `excalidraw-app/collab/Collab.tsx:471-565`
2. **startup complete** in `initializeRoom()`  
   `excalidraw-app/collab/Collab.tsx:708-752`

That gives you start + completion/fallback coverage without changing behavior.

### Avoid as primary hooks
- **`Portal.open()` / socket listeners** (`excalidraw-app/collab/Portal.tsx:37-60`)  
  Too close to ordering-sensitive protocol code.
- **`isCollaboratingAtom` / `activeRoomLinkAtom`** (`Collab.tsx:98-110`, `505`, `703`)  
  These flip **optimistically**, before startup is actually complete.
- **`App.initializeScene()`** (`excalidraw-app/App.tsx:216-372`)  
  Mixes collab and non-collab bootstrap logic.

### Rule to keep it behavior-safe
Make instrumentation:
- fire-and-forget
- non-`await`ed
- exception-swallowed
- state-free

So: **best completion hook = `initializeRoom()`; best outer lifecycle hook = `startCollaboration()`**. If you want, I can sketch the exact probe points.

fixtures.scenarioDescription: Provider-neutral eyedropper preview bug-fix task against the real Excalidraw repo

timelineRows.text: There's a positioning bug in the eyedropper preview: near the viewport edges, the preview can end up off-screen instead of flipping to the other side of the pointer. Please fix the positioning so the preview stays visible inside the viewport and add focused coverage for the edge cases.

Fixed.

What changed:
- `packages/excalidraw/components/EyeDropper.tsx`
  - Replaced the hardcoded `clientX + 20 / clientY + 20` positioning.
  - Now computes preview placement against the viewport, flips left/up near right/bottom edges, and clamps inside the viewport as a fallback.
  - Uses the preview’s measured size plus `visualViewport`/`innerWidth`/`innerHeight`.

- `packages/excalidraw/components/EyeDropper.common.ts`
  - Added a small pure helper: `getEyeDropperPreviewPosition()`.

- `packages/excalidraw/components/EyeDropper.test.ts`
  - Added focused coverage for:
    - normal in-viewport placement
    - right-edge horizontal flip
    - bottom-edge vertical flip
    - clamping when the preview can’t fit on either side

Checks run:
- `./node_modules/.bin/vitest run packages/excalidraw/components/EyeDropper.test.ts` ✅
- `./node_modules/.bin/eslint --max-warnings=0 packages/excalidraw/components/EyeDropper.tsx packages/excalidraw/components/EyeDropper.common.ts packages/excalidraw/components/EyeDropper.test.ts` ✅
Explain how the old positioning logic failed and how your regression coverage would have caught it.

The old logic failed because it always did this on every move:

- `left = clientX + 20`
- `top = clientY + 20`

That means it never considered:

- the preview’s own width/height
- the viewport width/height
- whether the preview should flip to the opposite side
- whether it should clamp when neither side has enough room

So near the viewport edges it could render off-screen.

Examples from the new tests:

- Right edge:
  - pointer at `x=280`, viewport width `300`, preview width `48`
  - old logic put `left=300`
  - preview then spans `300..348`, so it disappears off the right side

- Bottom edge:
  - pointer at `y=220`, viewport height `240`, preview height `48`
  - old logic put `top=240`
  - preview spans `240..288`, so it disappears below the viewport

- Tiny viewport / impossible fit:
  - old logic still used `+20/+20`, which can leave the whole preview outside the visible area
  - there was no fallback

How the regression coverage would catch it:

1. **Normal placement test**
   - verifies we still keep the preview on the default lower-right side when it fits

2. **Right-edge flip test**
   - expects the preview to move to the left of the pointer
   - old code would fail because it would still return `clientX + 20`

3. **Bottom-edge flip test**
   - expects the preview to move above the pointer
   - old code would fail because it would still return `clientY + 20`

4. **Clamp test**
   - expects the preview to stay within the viewport even when it can’t fit cleanly on either side
   - old code would fail because it never clamps

So the new tests directly encode the bug: if someone reintroduced the old fixed-offset logic, at least the edge-flip and clamp tests would fail immediately.

fixtures.scenarioDescription: Provider-neutral bug-fix task against the real Excalidraw repo

timelineRows.text: There's a usability bug in the canvas search sidebar: if I navigate to a later match and then change the query so fewer matches remain, focus can end up pointing at nothing. Fix it so a query change resets focus to the first match when matches exist, and add a regression test validating the behavior.

Fixed.

Changes:
- In `packages/excalidraw/components/SearchMenu.tsx`, query updates now set focus to the first match (`0`) whenever matches exist, otherwise `null`.
- Added a regression test in `packages/excalidraw/tests/search.test.tsx` for:
  - search multiple matches
  - navigate to a later one
  - change the query so only an earlier match remains
  - verify focus resets to that first remaining match

Verified with:
- `./node_modules/.bin/vitest run packages/excalidraw/tests/search.test.tsx`
- `./node_modules/.bin/eslint packages/excalidraw/components/SearchMenu.tsx packages/excalidraw/tests/search.test.tsx`
Explain why the regression would have failed before your change.

Before the change, the search input’s `onChange` handler did this:

1. recomputed matches
2. took `index` from `handleSearch(...)`
3. called `setFocusIndex(index)`

The problem is that `handleSearch()` did **not** return “first match for the new query.” It returned the **first currently visible match in the viewport**:

```ts
const focusIndex = matchItems.findIndex((matchItem) =>
  visibleIds.has(matchItem.element.id),
);
```

So after you had navigated to a later result, the canvas was scrolled there. When the query changed:

- the viewport was still centered on that later area
- the narrowed results might no longer include anything visible there
- `findIndex(...)` returned `-1`

Before my fix, that `-1` was stored as the focus index. Then the focus effect ran and:

- `matches[-1]` produced no `focusedId`
- no item got `focus: true`

So the sidebar ended up with matches present, but **nothing focused**.

That’s exactly what the regression test sets up:
- start with 3 matches
- navigate to the 3rd one
- change the query so only the 1st match remains
- before the fix, the remaining match is offscreen, so old code would leave focus empty instead of resetting to the first result.

fixtures.scenarioDescription: Provider-neutral explanation task against the real Excalidraw repo

timelineRows.text: I'm trying to understand Excalidraw's text-to-diagram flow before changing it. Please trace the flow from the dialog UI through chat history/state updates to the code that turns the final response into scene updates. Call out the main files, the key types, and any tricky state transitions or failure cases. Keep it grounded in the current codebase with specific file references.

Here’s the current end-to-end flow I found.

### 1) Entry points / dialog mounting
- In the app wrapper, `excalidraw-app/App.tsx:1019-1021` mounts both `AIComponents` and `TTDDialogTrigger`.
- Clicking the trigger calls `setAppState({ openDialog: { name: "ttd", tab: "text-to-diagram" } })` in `packages/excalidraw/components/TTDDialog/TTDDialogTrigger.tsx:23-31`.
- `openDialog` is typed in `packages/excalidraw/types.ts:388-392`.
- `TTDDialog` itself lives in `packages/excalidraw/components/TTDDialog/TTDDialog.tsx:26-133`. It:
  - checks `appState.openDialog?.name === "ttd"` (`:36-42`)
  - lazy-loads `@excalidraw/mermaid-to-excalidraw` (`:70-82`)
  - renders the text-to-diagram tab and the Mermaid tab (`:95-131`).

One subtlety: `LayerUI` has a fallback `<TTDDialog __fallback />` at `packages/excalidraw/components/LayerUI.tsx:483`, but the real app overrides it with the host-mounted `TTDDialog` from `AIComponents`; fallback suppression is handled by `withInternalFallback` in `packages/excalidraw/components/hoc/withInternalFallback.tsx:53-67`.

### 2) Core dialog state
The shared Jotai state is in `packages/excalidraw/components/TTDDialog/TTDContext.tsx:7-17`:
- `chatHistoryAtom`
- `rateLimitsAtom`
- `showPreviewAtom`
- `errorAtom`

Key types are in `packages/excalidraw/components/TTDDialog/types.ts`:
- `LLMMessage` (`:11-14`)
- `TChat.ChatMessage` / `TChat.ChatHistory` (`:26-46`)
- `TTTDDialog.OnTextSubmitProps` / `OnTextSubmitRetValue` (`:84-106`)
- `TTDPersistenceAdapter` (`:62-72`)

### 3) UI submit flow -> chat history updates
`TextToDiagram` wires everything together in `packages/excalidraw/components/TTDDialog/TextToDiagram.tsx:50-80`.

The input box is in `packages/excalidraw/components/TTDDialog/Chat/ChatInterface.tsx:73-86`:
- submit trims `currentPrompt`
- calls `onGenerate({ prompt })`
- clears `currentPrompt`

The main state machine is `packages/excalidraw/components/TTDDialog/hooks/useTextGeneration.ts:66-223`:
1. validate prompt (`:42-64`)
2. abort any prior stream (`:74-81`)
3. normal flow:
   - append user message
   - append empty assistant placeholder  
   via `useChatAgent` (`packages/excalidraw/components/TTDDialog/Chat/useChatAgent.ts:11-32`)
4. repair/retry flow:
   - **mutates the last assistant message in place** instead of adding a new visible turn (`useTextGeneration.ts:83-96`)
5. build messages for the backend from chat history:
   - `getMessagesForLLM(chatHistory)` in `packages/excalidraw/components/TTDDialog/utils/chat.ts:77-92`
   - then only keeps the last 3 previous messages and appends the new user prompt (`useTextGeneration.ts:101-106`)
6. stream chunks into the last assistant message using `updateAssistantContent()` (`useTextGeneration.ts:122-129`, helper in `utils/chat.ts:5-33`)
7. mark `isGenerating: false` on finish (`useTextGeneration.ts:133-137`)
8. parse-check the final Mermaid with `parseMermaidToExcalidraw()` (`:193-203`)

Important contract detail: the UI content is built from `onChunk`, not from `generatedResponse`. So a custom `onTextSubmit` that only returns `{ generatedResponse }` but never calls `onChunk` won’t populate the assistant bubble.

### 4) Current app’s backend boundary
The current app injects `onTextSubmit` in `excalidraw-app/components/AI.tsx:104-122`. It uses `TTDStreamFetch()` against:

- `${VITE_APP_AI_BACKEND}/v1/ai/text-to-diagram/chat-streaming`

The client-side streaming/fetch code is `packages/excalidraw/components/TTDDialog/utils/TTDStreamFetch.ts:84-225`:
- POSTs `{ messages }`
- expects SSE `data:` lines containing JSON chunks of type:
  - `"content"`
  - `"done"`
  - `"error"` (`:19-34`)
- accumulates `fullResponse`
- forwards deltas through `onChunk`
- extracts rate-limit headers (`:36-46`, `:111-113`)

The actual server prompt/model logic is **not in this repo**; the repo stops at this fetch boundary.

### 5) Final response -> preview rendering
The preview path watches the **last assistant message** in `packages/excalidraw/components/TTDDialog/hooks/useMermaidRenderer.ts:28-212`.

Key behavior:
- it throttles preview renders while streaming (`:18-21`, `:101-151`)
- it skips obviously incomplete Mermaid via `isValidMermaidSyntax()` (`packages/excalidraw/components/TTDDialog/utils/mermaidValidation.ts:1-40`)
- it converts Mermaid -> Excalidraw preview data through `convertMermaidToExcalidraw()` (`useMermaidRenderer.ts:64-99`)

That helper lives in `packages/excalidraw/components/TTDDialog/common.ts:43-131`:
- calls `parseMermaidToExcalidraw`
- converts returned elements via `convertToExcalidrawElements(..., { regenerateIds: true })` (`:97-105`)
- stores them into `data.current`
- renders preview canvas via `exportToCanvas()` (`:107-121`)

### 6) Final response -> actual scene updates
There are two insert paths:

#### A. Insert the currently previewed result
- `TextToDiagram.tsx:166-168` -> `insertToEditor({ app, data })`

#### B. Insert a historical assistant message directly
- `TextToDiagram.tsx:101-131`
- reconverts that message into a temp ref
- then calls `insertToEditor(...)`

`insertToEditor()` is in `packages/excalidraw/components/TTDDialog/common.ts:139-169`:
- reads `data.current.elements/files`
- calls `app.addElementsFromPasteOrLibrary({ position: "center", fitToContent: true })` (`:159-164`)
- closes the dialog (`:165`)

The scene mutation is in `packages/excalidraw/components/App.tsx:3878-4025`:
- restores elements (`:3885-3887`)
- computes bounds / center (`:3888-3915`)
- duplicates and re-seeds them (`:3916-3925`)
- **appends** them to the current scene, not replace-only (`:3927-3955`)
- adds files (`:3973-3975`)
- selects inserted elements (`:3977-4012`)
- scrolls to fit content (`:4021-4025`)

So the AI result is turned into scene updates by:
`assistant Mermaid text -> common.ts convertMermaidToExcalidraw -> common.ts insertToEditor -> App.addElementsFromPasteOrLibrary`.

### 7) Chat persistence / restore
Persistence is pluggable via `TTDPersistenceAdapter` (`types.ts:62-72`).

Current app uses IndexedDB in `excalidraw-app/data/TTDStorage.ts:11-50`.

`packages/excalidraw/components/TTDDialog/useTTDChatStorage.ts:83-183`:
- autosaves when the last message stops generating (`:145-155`)
- stores only `user` and `assistant` messages (`:114-116`)
- caps saved chats to 10 (`:129-134`)

`packages/excalidraw/components/TTDDialog/hooks/useChatManagement.ts:28-96`:
- restore converts timestamps back to `Date` (`:30-36`)
- resets `currentPrompt` to `""` on restore (`:38-42`)
- sets global error from the last assistant message (`:44-49`)
- deleting the active chat restores the most recent remaining chat or creates a new blank one (`:74-96`)

### 8) Tricky transitions / failure cases
The main ones I’d watch before changing this flow:

- **Repair/retry rewrites the last assistant message in place**  
  `useTextGeneration.ts:83-96`, `TextToDiagram.tsx:133-164`  
  No new visible user turn is added for AI repair or retry.

- **Only the last 3 prior messages go to the backend**  
  `useTextGeneration.ts:101-106`  
  The visible chat can be longer than the backend context.

- **Errored assistant content can still be sent back to the model**  
  `utils/chat.ts:77-92`  
  `getMessagesForLLM()` filters by type/content, not by `error`.

- **Abort leaves partial assistant content behind**  
  `useTextGeneration.ts:133-180`  
  It clears `isGenerating` before checking for abort, so a stopped stream can remain as partial Mermaid with no error.

- **429 leaves the user prompt in history**  
  `useTextGeneration.ts:143-169`  
  It removes only the assistant placeholder on 429, not the user turn.

- **Rate-limit warning dedupe looks wrong**  
  `useTextGeneration.ts:149-167`  
  The filter keeps existing rate-limit warnings, then appends another one, so warnings can stack.

- **Delete on an assistant message truncates the conversation branch**  
  `TextToDiagram.tsx:170-184`  
  It slices to before the preceding user turn, not just that single message.

- **“Fix in Mermaid” uses localStorage as the handoff**  
  `TextToDiagram.tsx:82-99`, `common.ts:132-137`, `MermaidToExcalidraw.tsx:70-74`  
  It does not pass Mermaid text through shared chat state.

- **Preview conversion and final parse validation are slightly different code paths**  
  validation: `useTextGeneration.ts:193-203`  
  preview/insert conversion: `common.ts:73-95`  
  The preview helper has an extra quote-replacement retry that the validation step doesn’t.

- **Closing the dialog does not reset chat state**  
  `TTDDialog.tsx:85-89`  
  Reopening resumes the same `chatHistoryAtom` unless the user explicitly starts a new chat.

If you want, I can turn this into a compact call graph or annotate the exact functions you’d likely touch for a behavior change.
What's the safest extension point if I want to tweak the UI without changing the scene-generation logic?

Safest **public** extension point: the `TTDDialog` render props.

- Defined in `packages/excalidraw/components/TTDDialog/types.ts:108-124`
  - `renderWelcomeScreen`
  - `renderWarning`
- Passed through in `packages/excalidraw/components/TTDDialog/TTDDialog.tsx:116-121`
- Used here:
  - welcome screen: `packages/excalidraw/components/TTDDialog/Chat/ChatInterface.tsx:113-119`
  - warning messages: `packages/excalidraw/components/TTDDialog/Chat/ChatMessage.tsx:65-106`

Why this is safest:
- it only changes rendering
- it does **not** touch chat state updates, Mermaid parsing, preview generation, or scene insertion

If you need a bit more UI customization inside the repo, the next-safest layer is the **presentational components**:

- `packages/excalidraw/components/TTDDialog/Chat/TTDChatPanel.tsx`
- `packages/excalidraw/components/TTDDialog/Chat/ChatInterface.tsx`
- `packages/excalidraw/components/TTDDialog/Chat/ChatMessage.tsx`
- `packages/excalidraw/components/TTDDialog/TTDPreviewPanel.tsx`
- `packages/excalidraw/components/TTDDialog/TTDDialogPanel.tsx`

Those mostly render props/callbacks coming from higher-level orchestration.

Files I’d avoid if your goal is “UI only”:
- `packages/excalidraw/components/TTDDialog/hooks/useTextGeneration.ts`
- `packages/excalidraw/components/TTDDialog/hooks/useMermaidRenderer.ts`
- `packages/excalidraw/components/TTDDialog/common.ts`
- `packages/excalidraw/components/App.tsx:3878-4025` (`addElementsFromPasteOrLibrary`)

So: **best answer = use `TTDDialog`’s render props first; if that’s not enough, limit edits to `Chat/*` + panel components and leave the hooks/common scene pipeline alone.**
